10 Fifth Avenue | "Thimble Theatre Dreams"


MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1]


[15] "Ten Fifth Avenue, Gothic townhouse on the block" → Inspired by: "10 Fifth Avenue (21) is an understated gem. This Gothic townhouse, one of a group of four built in 1848 by Henry Brevoort Jr." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 27.

[6] "Eighteen-forty-nine, brownstone front and crenellated top" → Drawn from: "No. 10 Fifth Avenue (1848-1849) at the northwest corner of West Eighth Street" and "some row houses of the late 1840s were among the first dwellings in the city to employ the then-daring full brownstone front." Lockwood. (1972) Bricks and Brownstone, p. 112.

[8] "Eighteen-forty-nine, brownstone front and crenellated top" → Supported by: "Was built in 1849."

Unknown Author. Landmarks Preservation Commission, p. 1.

[2] "Henry Brevoort built it, first man on the avenue" → Drawn from: "Old Henry Brevoort was the first

man to live on Fifth Avenue." Patterson. (1998) Fifth Avenue The Best Address, p. 9.

[15] "Henry Brevoort built it, first man on the avenue" → Supported by: "one of a group of four built in

1848 by Henry Brevoort Jr." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 27.

[15] "Quatrefoil decoration, stone panels in the view" → Directly from: "features a crenellated cornice, fine window frames and mullions, and stone panels with quatrefoil decoration above the windows on the second floor." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 27.

[2] "Minetta Water flooding basements down below" → Drawn from: "Through the entire low-lying area ran Minetta Water, a creek that was always ducking beneath the surface and which, even after the area had been drained and Fifth Avenue laid out, continued to flood basements." Patterson. (1998) Fifth Avenue The Best Address, p. 9.

[2] "His father was a mariner, fortune made at sea you know" → Directly from: "because," as he wrote in his will, "my father was a mariner; his fortune was made at sea." Patterson. (1998) Fifth Avenue The Best Address, p. 9.

[Chorus]


[16] "Ten Fifth Avenue, where the ghosts still play" → Inspired by: "Home to the ghost of the theatre's

founder, Guido Bruno." Lanigan-Schmidt. (2003) Ghosts of New York City, p. 64.

[16] "Music from no source, objects sail away" → Directly from: "peculiar happenings, such as music playing from no visible source and objects sailing across the room." Lanigan-Schmidt. (2003) Ghosts of New York City, p. 64.

[2] "From pirate gold to phonographs, opera in the square" → Inspired by: "actually, the elder Randall

had been a successful pirate in the Caribbean." Patterson. (1998) Fifth Avenue The Best Address, p. 9.

[19] "From pirate gold to phonographs, opera in the square" → Supported by: "Strollers unaccustomed to music without musicians were startled out of their wits by the opera overtures blaring from the bushes." Miller. (1990) Greenwich Village and how it got that way, p. 69.

[6] "Brownstone on the corner, legends layered there" → Drawn from: "No. 10 Fifth Avenue (1848-1849) at the northwest corner of West Eighth Street." Lockwood. (1972) Bricks and Brownstone, p. 112.

[Verse 2]


[16] "Guido Bruno in his plaid suits, flashy and ill-fit" → Directly from: "Guido Bruno, a Greenwich Village eccentric usually identified by his flashy, ill-fitting plaid suits." Lanigan-Schmidt. (2003) Ghosts of New York City, p. 64.

[19] "Thimble Theatre in the basement, wooden benches lit" → Drawn from: "By October 1915 he had installed wooden benches to seat one hundred and a stage equipped with curtains." Miller. (1990) Greenwich Village and how it got that way, p. 69.

[19] "Strindberg's Miss Julie, first performance in the land" → Drawn from: "the Bruno Players earned theatrical immortality with the first regular performances in America of Strindberg's brutal drama, 'Miss Julie'." Miller. (1990) Greenwich Village and how it got that way, p. 69.

[19] "Peacock curtains by Clara Tice, butterflies so grand" → Directly from: "curtains decorated by Clara Tice with butterflies and peacocks in attitudes more chaste than her earlier designs for Bruno." Miller. (1990) Greenwich Village and how it got that way, p. 69.

[19] "Edison's son cranked the phonograph, strollers lost their minds" → Drawn from: "Charles Edison had been trying without success to sell the phonograph records his famous father had invented" and "with him planned a promotional stunt that involved playing a hand-cranked phonograph in Washington Square. Strollers unaccustomed to music without musicians were startled out of their wits." Miller. (1990) Greenwich Village and how it got that way, p. 69.

[10] "Music blaring from the bushes, no musicians you could find" → Directly from: "Strollers were surprised to hear music coming out of the bushes with no musicians to be seen." Dickhuth. (2011) Greenwich Village, p. 120.

[Bridge]


  1. "Brick or stone first class in eighteen-fifty-three" → Directly from: "In the 1853 Perris Maps, on 10 Fifth Avenue, there is reference to 1 Structure; Brick or Stone dwellings first class." Perris. 1853 Perris Maps, p. 62.

  1. "Slate or metal roof, skylights setting top floors free" → Drawn from: "slate or metal roof &

    coped...with skylights lighting the top floor only." Perris. 1853 Perris Maps, p. 62.

    [5] "Five stories by nineteen-sixteen, the block kept climbing high" → Drawn from: "In the 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan, on 10 Fifth Avenue, there is reference to 1 Structure; 5 Story Brick building." G W Bromley & Co. 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 43.

    [6] "Northwest corner Eighth Street where the legends never die" → Drawn from: "No. 10 Fifth Avenue (1848-1849) at the northwest corner of West Eighth Street." Lockwood. (1972) Bricks and Brownstone, p. 112.

    [Outro]


    [15] "Ten Fifth Avenue standing, understated gem" → Directly from: "10 Fifth Avenue (21) is an

    understated gem." Hennessey. (2022) Fifth Avenue, p. 27.

  2. "From village to metropolis, I tip my hat to them" → Drawn from: "1 From Village to Metropolis."

Patterson. (1998) Fifth Avenue The Best Address, p. 9.
